11 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

🔗 Live Demo

Every Angular developer eventually hits the same wall. You know RxJS has operators for what you need. You’ve read the docs. You’ve seen marble diagrams. But when you’re staring at a real problem — a search input, a form submission, a dashboard loading three API calls — you freeze.
“Do I use switchMap or mergeMap? Is this a combineLatest or a forkJoin? Where does catchError go?"
This article is the decision guide I wish I had when I started leaning RxJS. It covers three categories of operators — flattening, combination, and error handling — and provides a decision framework for choosing the right one in every situation.
No marble diagrams. No abstract theory. Just practical decisions backed by interactive demos.

Part 1: The Flattening Decision — “I Need to Make an Async Call”
This is the most common decision Angular developers face. You have a stream of user actions — search queries, button clicks, route changes — and each action triggers an async operation like an HTTP call.
The question isn’t whether to use a flattening operator. The question is which one.
The Four Strategies
All four flattening operators do the same basic thing: they receive a value, create an inner Observable (like an HTTP call), and flatten the result into the output stream. The difference is what happens when a new value arrives while a previous inner Observable is still running.
switchMap — The TV Remote
You’re channel surfing. You press Channel 5. While it’s loading, you press Channel 8. The TV immediately stops loading Channel 5 and switches to Channel 8.

• Cancels the previous inner Observable

• Only the latest result matters

• Previous in-flight requests are terminated
When to use it:

• Search typeahead — you don’t want results for “ang” when the user already typed “angular”

• Route parameter changes — navigating from /product/1 to /product/2 should cancel the fetch for product 1

• Polling — each new poll should replace the previous one
searchInput$.pipe(
debounceTime(300),
distinctUntilChanged(),
switchMap(query => this.http.get(`/search?q=${query}`))
)mergeMap — The Multi-Screen Sports Bar
Ten TVs. Every game plays simultaneously. Nobody cancels anything. Every screen runs independently.

• Runs all inner Observables in parallel

• Nothing is cancelled, nothing is queued

• Every emission is processed independently
When to use it:

• Analytics logging — every event must be recorded, regardless of what else is happening

• Fire-and-forget operations — sending a notification, logging an action

• File processing — every file should be processed independently
fileList$.pipe(
mergeMap(file => this.uploadService.upload(file))
)concatMap — The Checkout Queue
One customer at a time. If a new customer arrives while the current one is being served, they wait in line. Strict order. Nobody gets skipped.

• Runs inner Observables one at a time, in order

• New emissions are queued until the current one completes

• Preserves order — results arrive in the same sequence as the source
When to use it:

• Sequential file uploads — file 2 must start after file 1 finishes

• Ordered database writes — insert parent record before child records

• Chat messages — messages must be sent in the order they were typed
messageQueue$.pipe(
concatMap(msg => this.chatService.send(msg))
)exhaustMap — The Busy Bouncer
The bouncer is checking someone’s ID. New people walk up. The bouncer completely ignores them until the current check is finished.

• Ignores new emissions while the current inner Observable is active

• No cancellation, no queuing — new values are simply dropped

• Prevents duplicate concurrent operations
When to use it:

• Login button — clicking “Login” 5 times rapidly should only trigger one login attempt

• Form submission — prevent duplicate submissions while the first one is processing

• Refresh button — ignore rapid clicks while data is loading
loginButton$.pipe(
exhaustMap(() => this.authService.login(credentials))
)The Flattening Decision Framework
When you need to choose a flattening operator, ask yourself one question:
“What should happen to the previous request when a new one arrives?”

• Cancel it → switchMap

• Let it finish alongside the new one → mergeMap

• Let it finish, then start the new one → concatMap

• Ignore the new one until the current finishes → exhaustMap
In Reactive Lab, I built a side-by-side comparison where all four operators process the same input simultaneously. You click “Emit Task” three times quickly and watch:

• switchMap: Task A ❌, Task B ❌, Task C ✅

• mergeMap: Task A ✅, Task B ✅, Task C ✅

• concatMap: Task A ✅, Task B ✅, Task C ✅ (but sequentially — 6 seconds total)

• exhaustMap: Task A ✅, Task B 🚫, Task C 🚫
Same input. Four completely different outputs.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Same input, four different outcomes. switchMap cancelled two tasks,
mergeMap completed all three in parallel, concatMap queued them
sequentially, and exhaustMap ignored two while the first was busy.
Part 2: The Combination Decision — “I Have Multiple Streams”
Real Angular apps have multiple independent data sources — a user stream, a notifications stream, a settings stream — and they need to be orchestrated.
The Three Strategies
combineLatest — The Recipe
You’re making a sandwich. You need bread, cheese, and lettuce. The sandwich is only “ready” when you have the latest version of all ingredients. If someone swaps the cheese, the sandwich updates — but you still use the bread and lettuce that are already there.

• Waits until every source has emitted at least once

• After that, emits a new combined value whenever any source emits

• Always uses the latest value from each source
When to use it:

• Form validation — combine name$, email$, and terms$ to check if the entire form is valid

• Dashboard widgets — combine user$, notifications$, and status$ — UI updates when any piece changes

• Multi-filter UI — combine category$, priceRange$, and sortOrder$ to build a query
combineLatest([
this.nameControl.valueChanges,
this.emailControl.valueChanges,
this.termsCheckbox.valueChanges
]).pipe(
map(([name, email, terms]) => isFormValid(name, email, terms))
)An important behavior: combineLatest won't emit until all sources have emitted at least once.
In Reactive Lab, I built a waiting indicator that shows which sources have emitted and which haven’t — making this “waiting for all sources” behavior explicitly visible.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

combineLatest won’t emit until all three sources have produced
at least one value. The waiting indicator makes this behavior
explicitly visible.merge — The News Ticker
A news ticker at the bottom of a TV screen. The ticker doesn’t care where a story comes from; it can be from sports, politics, or weather. It shows them as they arrive, interleaved.

• Subscribes to all sources simultaneously

• Emits values from any source as they arrive

• No waiting, no combining — just interleaving
When to use it:

• Multiple action buttons — merge(saveClick$, deleteClick$, cancelClick$) — handle all actions in one subscription

• Multiple WebSocket channels — merge(chatMessages$, systemAlerts$)

• Unifying event sources — merge(mouseClick$, touchTap$, keyboardEnter$)
merge(
this.saveBtn.click$.pipe(map(() => 'save')),
this.deleteBtn.click$.pipe(map(() => 'delete')),
this.cancelBtn.click$.pipe(map(() => 'cancel'))
).subscribe(action => this.handleAction(action))forkJoin — The Restaurant Table
You order steak. Your friend orders pasta. Another friend orders salad. The waiter doesn’t bring each dish as it’s ready — the waiter waits until all dishes are prepared, then brings them to the table together.

• Subscribes to all sources simultaneously (they run in parallel)

• Waits until every source completes

• Emits a single array or object with the last value from each source
When to use it:

• Page initialization — forkJoin([loadUser(), loadPosts(), loadSettings()]) — show loading spinner until all complete

• Batch operations — forkJoin(files.map(f => uploadFile(f))) — wait for all uploads

• Parallel API calls — forkJoin({ user: getUser(), prefs: getPrefs() })
forkJoin({
user: this.http.get('/api/user'),
posts: this.http.get('/api/posts'),
settings: this.http.get('/api/settings')
}).subscribe(({ user, posts, settings }) => {
this.initDashboard(user, posts, settings);
})A critical architectural note from my Reactive Lab implementation: forkJoin requires its sources to complete. If you use long-lived Subjects, completing one for forkJoin would also complete it for any other operator listening to the same Subject. That's why I used separate Subjects for forkJoin — dedicated copies that only forkJoin listens to. Completing those copies doesn't affect the sources used by combineLatest or merge.
The Combination Decision Framework
“How should multiple streams be combined?”

• Need the latest from ALL, updating on any change → combineLatest

• Just interleave values as they arrive → merge

• Wait for ALL to finish, then get final values → forkJoin
A simpler way to remember:

• combineLatest → ongoing — keeps emitting as sources change

• merge → pass-through — each value flows independently

• forkJoin → one-shot — emits once when everything finishes

Part 3: The Error Handling Decision — “What Happens When Things Break?”
Understanding flattening and combination operators is important, but if you don’t handle errors correctly, one failed HTTP call can kill your entire application’s reactive pipeline.
The Critical Rule
In RxJS, an unhandled error terminates the stream permanently. This is fundamentally different from try/catch in synchronous code, where execution continues after the catch block.
source$.pipe(
switchMap(query => this.http.get(`/search?q=${query}`))
).subscribe({
next: result => { ... },
error: err => {
// Stream is now DEAD
// Future emissions produce NOTHING
}
})The key to understanding RxJS error handling is recognizing it as an ownership problem. Just as component lifecycle, subscription lifecycle, and communication lifecycle are three separate concerns that must be managed independently, error handling requires a clear answer to one question: Who is responsible for recovering — the inner Observable or the outer stream?
WHERE You Put catchError Changes Everything
This is the single most important concept in RxJS error handling. In Reactive Lab, I built three panels to prove it:
catchError OUTSIDE switchMap — The Fragile Stream
source$.pipe(
switchMap(query => this.http.get(`/search?q=${query}`)),
catchError(err => of([]))
)The error escapes the inner Observable, reaches the outer stream, and catchError handles it — but the outer stream completes. The subscription is terminated. Future search queries produce nothing.
In the demo, the panel shows 💀 Dead after the first error. Clicking the Success button afterward does nothing — the stream is permanently terminated.
catchError INSIDE switchMap — The Resilient Stream
source$.pipe(
switchMap(query =>
this.http.get(`/search?q=${query}`).pipe(
catchError(err => of([]))
)
)
)The error is caught within the inner Observable’s lifecycle. The outer stream never sees it. Future queries work perfectly.
In the demo, the panel shows 🛡️ Always Alive. Every error is handled per-request. The stream survives indefinitely.
retry + catchError — The Persistent Stream
source$.pipe(
switchMap(query =>
this.http.get(`/search?q=${query}`).pipe(
retry({ count: 3, delay: () => timer(500) }),
catchError(err => of([]))
)
)
)retry(3) resubscribes to the inner Observable up to 3 times when an error occurs. If all retries fail, catchError handles the final error gracefully. The outer stream stays alive either way.
In the demo, you can watch the Activity Log show retry attempts 1/3, 2/3, 3/3 — then the final recovery.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Same error, three different outcomes. Th
